title: 2025/10/09 - TI
displayTitle: 2025-10-09 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Transport and Infrastructure Committe
description: 9 October 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committe Land Transport (Clean Vehicle Standard) Amendment Bill (No 2) Giltrap Motor Group Steve Kenchington, Giltrap Group CEO Greg Fleming, European Motor Distributors Homologation Manager Kurt Schmidt, Giltrap Group General Manager Finance Ford Motor Company of New Zealand Limited Annaliese Atina, Managing Director, Ford New Zealand Daniel Curtis, Director of Government Affairs, Australia and New Zealand Paul Altamore, Advanced Regulatory Manager, Australia and New Zealand Motor Trade Association Larry Fallowfield, Sector Manager Motor Industry Association Aimee Wiley, Chief Executive Hyundai Motors New Zealand Limited Leigthon Agnew, General Manager Sales Oliver Wilding, National Sales & Operations Planning Manager
descriptionSummary: Transport and Infrastructure Committe. Land Transport (Clean Vehicle Standard) Amendment Bill (No 2). Giltrap Motor Group

chunks.text: You here sincere while, okay. That's, that's better. Right? Okay. Good afternoon, gentlemen. Hi there. How we doing? Afternoon. Good to see you here. Um, look, we've got your, um, submission so you can take that as read. Uh, but what we'd like it if, um, is if you could, uh, speak to the points you wanna make. Um, I'll give you a yell when it's about three minutes to go.

We've got you 10 minutes, but we do like to ask questions. So, um, the more time you give us questions, the more likely we are to ask lots of questions, so in your hands. Thank you. Okay. So ki good afternoon, select committee chair and, uh, members, firstly, thank you. Thank you for the work that you're doing, um, that you're putting into this review. Thanks also for the opportunity to present this afternoon.

I really appreciate it. So with me here, I've got Steve Kensington, uh, he is the Gil Trapp Group, CEO. I've got Kurt Schmid, he's the Gil Trap Group, general Manager of Finance, and myself and Greg European Motor Distributors, uh, homologation and Business Quality Manager. So we are here on behalf of the Gil Trapp Group. Uh, one of New Zealand's largest automotive organizations employing around a thousand people across our importer and retail operations.

We represent 20 automotive brands, um, from affordable high volume, uh, vehicles through to aspirational marks. And we are very closely connected to a wide network of dealer partners and our collective customers. Clean Vehicle Standard already has a significant impact on our brands and the businesses that we represent, uh, and on the customers that we serve.

Also, that impact will further in 2026 as tighter targets take effect and the proposed amendments will compound it even more. Our focus today is on ensuring the standard continues to reduce emissions in a way that's fair, practical, and sustainable for all key reefs. The Clean Vehicle standard plays an important role in reducing fleet CO2 emissions, but it has to be fair, it has to be workable and it has to encourage genuine decarbonization over the long term.

It cannot distort the market or load costs unevenly on certain kiwis who have a genuine need for a specific vehicle. At present, the Clean Vehicle Standard is struggling to cut emissions as it was designed to, uh, because it only regulates supply without a mechanism to drive cus consumer demand, the system cannot deliver the shift in CO2 that it was intended to.

As outlined in our submission, we support extending the life of credits to four years. This increases certainty and flexibility. We also support extending borrowing provisions beyond 2025. This gives importers the confidence to plan. However, we remain opposed to opening credit transfers between new and used importers, uh, at a two for one exchange rate.

This has the potential to create arbitrage and to undermined investment in lower emission vehicles and fair competition. It could also drive up the price of credits making compliance harder and more expensive for new importers and their customers. We also remain strongly opposed to the removal of weight adjusted targets or the removal of the legislative requirement to factor vehicle weight into CO2 targets.

Weight adjustment ensures fairness. Small vehicles compared with small vehicles and large vehicles with large vehicles and targets are set based on the workload capability of the vehicle. Removing weight adjustment will result in significant cost increase that will be passed on to consumers.

This will punish families and businesses who genuinely need larger vehicles while simultaneously stripping away credits from EVs. Now to get a little technical, the positive correlation between CO2 and vehicle weight known as the slope of the line will shift to negative at relatively low EV uptake.

But true balance, when EV credits can offset debits doesn't occur until EVs make up a far higher percentage of sales. Removing weight adjustment at the slope of the line flip point would therefore punish families and businesses for years before consumer demand for EVs enough is enough to be give genuine balance.

So in closing, we support flexibility measures that make the system more workable, providing a clear pathway to carbon reduction. We strongly urge the committee to retain weight adjusted targets to preserve fairness and credibility, to reconsider credit transfers between new and used to prevent the possibility of market gaming and distortion.

And to consider implementing appropriate demand-based mechanisms. To balance the clean vehicle standard, we need a clean vehicle standard that actually reduces emissions, not one that distorts the market, punishes families and incentivizes the retention of older higher emission vehicles.

Thank you. And we can take any questions that you have, Greg. Okay, my first question from Dan. Hey, thanks Mr. Chair. Thanks Greg for your submission. Um, just in your recommendations, 0.7, you mentioned realigning New Zealand's climate ambitions to follow tests and proven programs in other markets. Could you explain what you mean by that?

Um, and yeah, definitely in particularly the other markets. Thank you, minister. Um, yeah, so there are, there are markets around the world that have, um, slightly better proven ambitions in this space. Um, we get the feeling that in New Zealand we're trying to be a little bit, um, ambitious ahead of where the market actually is and where, um, Kiwi's demands for low emission vehicles are.

Uh, and this is creating a little bit of a disparity. So, um, slowing down a little bit, perhaps following Australia or some of the other markets that, uh, um, are moving a little more deliberately and slowly, I think would be, would be advisable. So, Australia, any, any, any other specific market examples that we should be looking at?

Um, Australia is probably the, the one that aligns mostly with, um, Kiwi situation. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. So it just in simple terms, and we've heard this already today, what is, what you're saying that the, the vehicles that are available are not meeting the standard? If you go across the, all the nature of the different types of vehicles there are.

'cause not everybody wants a, you know, a, a very small car. Um, uh, the, the, the availability of those vehicles, um, does not match the, the standards which are being expected. I, I'd probably suggest more that the demand of New Zealanders doesn't match what the regulation or what the standard is trying to achieve. Okay, well that, well that means, yeah.

So effectively what that means is your, for you will be, if, if New Zealand's had to follow that, we'd be trying to force people into vehicles that are not necessarily the vehicles that they want. Yeah, correct. Yeah. So the, the other thing which we've heard, um, earlier, I just want your, um, observations on this is that there are now, uh, we're somewhat awash, I think was the word, which was used with, um, really small cars.

Um, but we're kind of running out of people movers. Is that, is that fair reflection? In other words, the, the market basically we've imported all these vehicles. Um, people said we want, we wanna buy, we wanna buy a whole lot of people movers. Uh, we're not so interested in the smaller cars, so now the smaller cars are sitting on the lots, but the people movers have all gone.

Steve, I could probably get you to comment on that a we bit. Um, I I'm not simplifying, but Yeah, I I'm not suggesting I I wouldn't suggest they were actually seeing that. Um, um, I, I don't think there is the demand for small cars. I think there's the, there's the demand for small cars in heavily congested metropolitan areas, but I don't think there is, um, outside of, um, potentially Auckland, maybe Wellington, um, to a lesser degree, but definitely the SUV sector is one that continues to grow.

Um, and you know, what we are seeing is coming out of the manufacturers is more hybrid, super hybrid technology that is heavier, um, which, you know, does have a negative, a correlation to what's tr what's being proposed. So, you know, I think, you know, we, I think we are moving towards a lower emission environment, um, but not, not maybe as quickly as we, um, you know, would like, and the manufacturers are dictating that to us in terms of what they're gonna build.

I think that's what, so thank you for that. I'll, I'll take Julianne and Carlos and we've got about two and a bit minutes left. Thank you so much for your submission. Um, I was wondering if you could expand on recommendation six, which is introducing demand focused initiatives to steer consumer choice. Um, I mean, do you think it would be helpful if there was some sort of price incentive, uh, such as there was with the clean car discount, which helped, um, which had quite a substantive impact on consumer demand?

Uh, I think far greater so that actually the importers were overshooting the targets when it came to the standard for the first few years. But now that there's nothing there do, are there other things government could do, like using government procurement for example, to, um, accelerate or increase the demand for, uh, low emissions vehicles that would help, uh, you balance the imports?

Thank you. Uh, yes, we, we would suggest that there are, I as a group, we don't really think that the clean vehicle discount was the right mechanism, but there are some that could be considered. Um, we can, looking at the moment at the, the rux across the board, so road user charges, um, as opposed to fuel taxes, um, that could be prorated, uh, based on CO2 output of the, of the vehicle.

Uh, another possibility would be vehicle licensing. Um, there could be a, a charger vehicle licensing, and this way we capture everyone who is contributing to CO2, not just the person who is, is buying a late model low machine vehicle. Um, so there's a couple po couple of possibilities that would help to, um, steer consumer demand. I Think we also did see a bit of a sugar rush, um, initially with the, um, with the incentives to purchase ev.

Um, and I say that because, um, you know, when those were taken away, we were actually selling vehicles well below, um, what the incentive was on a vehicle. Um, and, and sales were particularly hard to come by. So the, the customer was potentially significantly better off, um, on a true retail price with the, with the supply exceeding demand.

And um, you know, we still struggled to sell, um, EVs into the marketplace. So, so I do think that, I think it was sort of misaligned in terms of we saw a, we saw a big impact to start with and then it fell off quite quickly. I think I Hit a bit of a cap. Yeah, look, I think folks we're gonna, um, say have to say thank you there 'cause we're gonna move on to our, our next minute, but we, uh, you're very welcome to stay online and we appreciated that.

Cool. Thank you. Thank you Carlos. We'll go next time. Would you first up? Sure. Okay. Right. Uh, now we have got Ford Motor Company again via Zoom. Good afternoon. Good afternoon. I am Elise. Hello. Right again, uh, we've got you down for 10 minutes.

Um, you can take your submission as read anything you wanna highlight, go for it. I'll let you know when there's about three minutes to go. Um, but we like asking questions, so, um, uh, if you can leave time for questions, we appreciate that. So good news. That'll be about four minutes and we'll leave six minutes for questions. Uh, so first I queue everyone, uh, Elise called Managing Director Board Motor Company New Zealand.

Uh, so firstly, thanks for the opportunity to discuss with your permission and the land transport clean vehicle Standard Amendment bill. Uh, we've been part of the New Zealand community for over 117 years. We're the oldest car vehicle brand here and have a proud heritage across our Tiera supporting families, businesses, and critical industries to grow. We employ over 120 staff directly as Ford Motor Company in New Zealand, but more importantly across Arta.

Our dealerships span all the way from Kerry all the way down to the beautiful bottom of the south island and Gore and support over 1800 families through, uh, through employment globally. Ford is committed to a sustainable future and we are pursuing very ambitious goals of being carbon neutral across our vehicles manufacturing facilities and supply chain.

By 2050 New Zealand's Clean Car Standard has sent a clear signal to the industry to introduce efficient and low emission vehicles to the market. And we are proud to offer a range of capable electrified vehicles to New Zealand consumers and businesses such as a Mackey SUV Eran van, and more recently the Ranger PHU.

However, our experience is that the Clean car standard exceeds the ability of the automotive sector to deliver the electrified vehicles that our customers need an uptake of full EVs still remain at five to 6%. We are concerned that the current approach will increase cost for consumers without lifting the speed that Ford or other manufacturers can supply low emission vehicles to the market.

We're asking the government to take concrete action to improve the operation of the Clean Car standard at no cost to the taxpayer, incentivizing more buyers to choose low or zero emissions vehicles. And we ask for the Parliament six things to consider establish New Zealand as a fast follower, adopting achievable emission actions from other markets with a delay of three years, incentivize fully electric and other low emitting technologies like PHE and emerging electrified segments like pickups and vans, which are, which are capable of greater work,

that it does not replace the current weight-based CO2 targets with uniform targets. These would penalize larger and more capable vehicles, including those serving critical functions for our communities and our businesses to treat off-road SUVs as commercial vehicles for the purposes of clean car standard, consistent with the US and with Australia that you proceed with extending the lifespan of credits from three to four years as proposed.

And lastly, introduce electrified vehicles. Sorry. Lastly include electric electrified vehicles, which are, which due to the extra weight of their battery fall outside of the current light commercial vehicle category with a scheme to earn credits. Today I'm joined by Paul Ultimore, our Australian Zealand regulatory team lead, and also Dan Curtis, who is our Director of Government Affairs Australia, New Zealand.

And we're happy to answer any questions from our submission. Thank you. Thanks Elise. Thanks Elise. Thank you all. We've got about just over six minutes, uh, questions folks. Carlos, do you wanna kick me off with one? Okay, Dan. Okay, Thanks Annalise. Um, we've heard in some of the submissions around the mismatch between, um, demand and supply.

I'm wondering if you can tell us from Ford's perspective, what, in terms of the import import of vehicles in New Zealand, how, what proportion of of yours are ev versus hybrid versus um, other standard? Yeah, I, I would say that, uh, when the scheme first kicked off, we had a strong mix into, uh, EV and hybrid over time.

That's obviously disseminated due to supply, but if, if we go back to where the consumers, uh, are buying, they are sitting, uh, largely at the EV is very small, again, it's only 6%. We're seeing a small swing into hybrid and that's really matching our portfolio. So think for Ford here and feel free, uh, port to jump in at any time.

Uh, we are seeing that demand, uh, in the categories that we play in, which is capable categories not really lift where we need it to be. And I think all manufacturers are feeling that across New Zealand, and that's not every segment. That's the segments we play within. Yeah, So, so just to be clear, you are seeing a misalignment between what, uh, the, um, clean car vehicle approach seems to want and what the market wants.

Yeah, there, there, there definitely isn't a misalignment and you can see that purely in the data. Um, and the challenge is the technology, and I think you've probably heard other manufacturers say the same thing. The technology for certain segments to do the work that Kiwis need to do is just not there, particularly in the right hand vehicle market. Okay, Cool. Um, thank you very much Elise.

I just have a question. Could you please, uh, expand on, uh, the opposition you have towards a weight-based CO2 target? Yeah, I might hand that one to Paul. Yeah, so, um, we are very keen to see the clean car stand re retain, um, uh, let's say, uh, an allowance for heavier vehicles to have higher allowable emissions that's consistent with these types of schemes that, um, operate around the world.

Um, and you know, that's what's used in Australia is what's used in the US though. It's of course it's based on footprint. And the reason for this is it clearly, um, puts a demarcation between vehicles that have higher capability and vehicles that have lower capability and weight is a good indicator of that. So we're very keen to keep the, um, weight dependent, um, targets, um, for, for vehicles in within the scheme and not to remove that to flatten the targets.

Good. Cool. Thank you. And I might just, I might just add to that the other key pieces for, and it's any manufacturer, right? So certainty is really important, particularly when you're not manufacturing product in country. And so when you've got uncertainty, which is what the bill is introducing, it makes it very difficult in terms of future product cycle planning because it's all based around certainty and that that's a large investment for traditional OEMs or manufacturers to consider.

Okay. Colleagues, other questions? Okay, well in that case I'll give myself the luxury of one more just, and, and we don't make, we don't make too many vehicles in New Zealand. Um, don't make too, we are, there's the old bus here, I think, but that's about it. Um, but um, in terms of, you've, you've suggested this being a fast follower, uh, who, what, what are you seeing in other markets around and obviously, um, Paul, you are, Paul at least you are based in in Australia.

Um, what are you seeing in other markets around, um, the world in terms of what they're acquiring and how that relates to the, uh, to, to the New Zealand standard and also to what manufacturers are actually able to supply? Yeah, I'm happy to happy to answer that. I think what we're seeing globally is a similar situation to what we're talking about here for New Zealand targets that are misaligned to the available technology, particularly in the high work capability segments that Ford operates in, whether it's our SUVs

or our pickup vehicles or our vans where about vehicles that can, can carry payloads, can move passengers flexibly, can tow and that sort of thing. And what we find is that the available electrification or lower emissions technology is still a number of years behind where the targets are for most developed markets around the world. So the misalignment we're seeing in New Zealand is not unique.

We talk about, um, adopting what other markets are doing with the delay that gives New Zealand the flexibility to adjust, um, with more, with a longer timeframe when say Europe makes a change or when the US makes a change. It also recognizes the fact that the vast majority of markets in the world where we're developing this technology for are left hand drive markets such as China, such as Europe, such as the us and that we have to, um, we need time to move those technologies into right hand drive platforms to make them available in markets like New Zealand.

So that's, that's why we feel like the, um, that while the initial scheme was quite aggressive and add, um, uh, quite a, uh, ambitious target to, to catch up or to move faster than some other, um, jurisdictions, it's important that New Zealand recognizes that it's, it's not a large market and that there's work manufacturers have to do to adapt vehicles for that environment.

Okay. And just to finish up, follow up that, and then I'll go to Julian. Um, so if we pursue the, their regime as is proposed at the moment, what does that start to look like in terms of the impact on your ability to be able to bring vehicles in or on what you've gotta pass on and or on what you've gotta pass on to customers in terms of added cost?

Well, if the, if the vehicles that we can supply to the market with the available technology, um, uh, and higher CO2 levels than the targets that, uh, legislate and there'll just be a gap in terms of in, in, in, in terms of what, um, our ability to comply with the standard. So that means cost to consumers, um, inability to trade effectively the trading system for us to move credits between manufacturers who are, have different technologies effectively locks up.

So there's no free credits available. And a lot of what we are talking about in our proposals is to free those credits up, um, as these newer technologies develop and become more available and more economically viable. And thanks Julian. Hey, just a really quick question. Um, just curious about why it is that, um, that technology is so different between right hand drive and left hand drive cars.

Like is there anything inherently about a right hand drive vehicle that would affect the ability to use fuel efficiency technology? It's really about when we develop a new technology and put into a new platform where we can put our investment dollars first and our investment dollars are always towards the biggest markets first, and then we will spread the investment out over time to make it developable in more marketplaces.

We can't invest 100% of what we need to invest to make it available everywhere in the world all at once. So we have to spread that investment out over years. So left hand drive, just because of the larger number of markets that use that technology, they get the investment first and then we have to adapt it into other markets where the requirements are different. So in the case of technology, sorry, it's not a case of the technology is not adaptable, it's our investment cycles to not allow us to just dump huge chunks of money in

for every market in the world all at once. But do you sell into uk Um, certain products? Yes, but it's, it's, it's no less challenging in the UK than other markets compared to EU 27. Look, thank you all very much. Really appreciate that. Thank you for having Us. Thank you.

Okay, now we've got the, uh, motor Trades Association, so welcome. Come, come to the table. Motor, motor Industry. Oh, hang on, I've got Motor Trades Association, oh, hang on, sorry. MTA, sorry, we've got, got so the wrong way. Uh, sorry. We should have the Motor Trades Association via Zoom.
Sorry, that's my mistake. Do you want me to go back? Oh, say stay there if you want to. Thank you. So do we have them there? Yep, there. Okay, cool. Good, right?

No, they're doing it. Good afternoon. Larry, could you turn your camera on and unmute yourself? There we go. That's better. Welcome. Sorry about that. Look, thanks Larry. We've got your submission so you can take that as read. Um, if you wanna highlight anything, that would be great.

We've got you down for 10 minutes. Uh, I'll give you a call with about three to go, um, unless if you're still speaking. Uh, but otherwise if you can leave time for questions, we really appreciate that and, uh, we'll get ourselves underway. Thank You. Thank you Mr. Chair. And thank you the select committee for giving us the opportunity to present here today. Uh, as mentioned, my name's Larry Fell Field.

I'm the sector manager for the Motor Trade Association, or MTA as we're known. Um, I look after the motor vehicle dealer space and we represent over 4,000 members across New Zealand and about 60,000 employees, uh, in the motor vehicle trader space. We have over 500 members ranging from new franchise to used Importers, motorcycle, motor home Caravan, and believe it or not, Marine.

So, um, look, thanks for the opportunity to, as I say to present here today, we've sort of raised a few different concerns and supportive of, of what our new franchise, um, partners have said, Gil Traps and Ford in, in that space. Um, we took a slightly different approach in looking at some of the challenges that are faced that ADD that may be amplified by this change.

And that was the fact that since its peak in 2017, over 900, um, registered motor vehicle traders have gone outta business or closed up. So about a seven 27% decrease, which is, um, which is, which is quite big. And that has continued to carry on. We've seen about 198 close so far this year.

Um, or whether that's from liquidation or just actually going, you know what, let's, let's pull out of this area 'cause it's not viable for us anymore. I suppose the thing in the used import space, we've seen a big decline in the numbers of used imports, uh, being sold. Uh, at the same time we've also seen an increase in the cost of, uh, uh, bringing in used imports and, and some of those costs of being around the fact of being able to get good cars.

Uh, given that a couple of years ago, uh, Japan put a ban on on Russia. Um, but since then we've seen a big uptake from the likes of Mongolia and UAE, so we're sort of competing. The other thing that's happened is, is quite, we're always compared to Australia, and yet our market is still slightly different.

Uh, Australia have very low volumes of imported vehicles. Uh, we have a high number of imported vehicles, but the Australians are starting to pick up in that space. To give you an example, in 2021, New Zealand sort of imported about, uh, 105,000 units. Uh, Australia was about 13,000.

And then in 24, um, new Zealand's dropped to 81,000, uh, and Australia's picked up to about 21. So we compete in that used import space in China, uh, sorry, in Japan against lots of players around the globe. And that puts pressure on getting vehicles here. So back to, uh, what we are seeing, and it probably reflective of what, uh, everybody else has mentioned so far this morning that, um, we're, we seen a big uptake with the clean car discount with EVs.

Uh, and that has come back to, to be sitting at about seven or 8% of the total, uh, vehicles being sold at the moment. Um, and it followed behind that across is the battery electric vehicles, uh, sorry, battery electric is, uh, PH EVs plugin hybrids. They're sitting at about 4%. And, and the big part of the market at the moment is obviously the, the hybrid electric vehicles.

Now they're currently sitting at about 38% of the market, which is a big jump from where we were probably five or six years ago. And I think we're in this transition period now where people are looking at, at hybrid electric vehicles as a stepping stone into the next category. So, um, a step from ICE or, or internal combustion, standard petrol diesel into that hybrid space is, is a, as I say, a transition period.

Once people get used to driving those, then potentially we'll see more people, um, uptake with PS and then battery electric vehicles from that space. But it's gonna take time to do that. And as um, and at lease from Ford mentioned, you know, there's, we New Zealand we're dominated by small to medium businesses.

And in that work, what we call, I suppose a good old workhorse, whether that's a Ford Ranger Ute or a Toyota Hilux are, are a big part of, um, what is used in, in that space in New Zealand. Um, and that technology is, is changing at a fast, rapid, not, uh, fast rate of knots, but not fast enough to get us to where we need to be.

Um, so some of our concerns were around that, the system that we had with the clean card discount, which, um, which helped drag themselves through perhaps advantaged some people and disadvantaged some others. And then as the, as the guys from general, uh, deal Traps mentioned when that discount came off, um, dealers were, and manufacturers were stuck with electric battery electric vehicles here in New Zealand that they tried to move at a lower price point better than what was being offered while the clean car discount was on.

Uh, but unfortunately it was just a slow uptake. Uh, for some reason I think people thought that getting money in the hand was better than actually the, the sale price of the vehicle or the purchase price of the vehicle. Um, so some of the things that, that we've sort of looked at as maybe the whole system needs to have a rethink. Um, our concern is that, uh, our current age of our vehicles in New Zealand is, is nearing 16 years old.

Um, if you compare that to places like the US sitting at probably 12 or 13 years, uh, the UK sits at around that nine to 10 year age gap. Um, and Australia sits at around between 11 and 12. And so how do we bring the age of those vehicles down, which is a, a key critical part of, uh, or should be a critical part of this ongoing discussion.

Um, I think some of the challenges we have is we think about cars that are 25 years old. Uh, and a stat I I recently sort of looked at sort of said that 40, nearly close to 50% of our vehicles are over that 15 year age gap. Um, if you think about a car that's a 19 or a 2000 year old, a 2000 year, uh, 25 years old now, it doesn't look any different than a modern vehicle.

Whereas if you were in the, in the year 2000 and we're looking at a car 25 years old, a 1975 car was a big difference in style. So we sort of have a, a, a view, I suppose, of what's around us that it looks actually perhaps older than it actually is. Um, from that side, Larry, you've got just over three minutes left.

Okay. So just quickly, I'll just finish up with what some of our recommendations were, uh, you know, and, and some of those were, uh, have been mentioned before, looking at rux, looking at time use of vehicle, uh, we see those as probably a more beneficial way of, of, uh, of looking at the clean car vehicles and reducing emissions versus, versus, um, trying to put a a, a cost increase on to, uh, to the consumer, which is what we think will happen should the, the program keep going in its current form.

Okay, thanks. Any, Any questions? Thank you, Larry. Just check to see are there any questions, colleagues? That all makes sense? No, look, I, I think, I think Larry, the, the challenge there is you've told us very similar things than we've heard from the other two, um, before you, uh, or the other three before you. And, um, and so I think we've, we've got that picture, so I really appreciate, I hope so because we do represent industry, so hopefully we're not too far off the mark with what they're saying. So Look, thank you very much.

You very much. Cheers. Bye-bye. Right. Amy, welcome. My name's Amy Wiley and I'm the Chief executive of the Motor Industry Association. Um, the Motor Industry Association represents all the new, all the new OEMs and quarters distributors and the retail networks. Uh, we've got 96 brands here in New Zealand now, and the my A represents, um, so that's approximately 99% of all new vehicles sold in the country, uh, spanning light vehicles, motorcycles, on and off road and heavies.

In terms of our, um, concerns around the clean vehicle standard, I wanna start by saying that the credit flexibility mechanisms are absolutely crucial. They formed part of an agreement and our advice back in the 2024 Clean, clean Car Standard Review, which is now called, called Clean Vehicle Standard. Um, and we've been waiting that the industry's been eagerly awaiting this, this bill for these changes because the system's really not functioning as intended.

And the clean car discount and the Clean Car standard were both brought in as tandem policies. And to use a human analogy, like a heart, a lung or a brain is a left and a right side. And at the moment with no, with no incentive on consumers to stimulate demand. And I'm not advocating to bring back a clean car discount, but the messaging isn't even there either.

So not only have we taken away, um, demand side stimulation, we've penalized electric vehicles by putting policy costs on top road user charges before the rest of the fleet that, um, a CC levies being redu exemption being reduced, everything has sent a signal to consumer sentiment that EVs that the total cost of ownership's not there anymore in terms of the benefit.

And actually now it's not the time for EVs, we're not worried about our missions anymore. And so with that sentiment, it's harder than ever for the new vehicle industry to shift these vehicles. And when the policy change came in with three months notice, um, there were thousands of cars stranded. These are cars that already had very tight margins because of high research and, um, r and d and, and a whole lot of costs to get them here to try and meet demand and uptake, which at the time was 23%.

In New Zealand we were breaking records globally at 23%. Now we're stuck at about five to 7%. And that's not unusual. I mean, Australia's got similar challenges. Canada's got similar challenges, Europe, us the whole lot, right? I hope that's not my time, that's Not your time. Sorry, that's me having not, not, uh, fixed the previous one. So, Right. Thank you.

Seven minutes. Seven minutes to go. But it's a really, it's a really big problem that's also combined in a really big economic challenging time for the country. So not only did they have to drop prices in order to try and shift these units, 'cause they couldn't stop the boats, they couldn't turn them around.
